1: Type — Eagle swooping against the house of the LORD — broken covenant brings the Assyrian invader like a bird of prey. The covenant structure — promise, demand, curse — finds its fulfillment in Christ who bears the covenant curse on Israel's behalf (Gal 3:13) so that the curse-eagle does not descend on those who are in him.
2: Direct — 'My God, we know you — we are Israel!' — confessional identity without relational reality. Matt 7:21-23: 'Not everyone who says to me "Lord, Lord" will enter the kingdom.' Jesus makes the same distinction between claimed belonging and genuine knowledge of God. True knowledge of God is personal, relational, and transformative — the kind Jesus brings.
3: Type — Israel throws away what is good; an enemy will pursue him — John 1:11: 'He came to his own, and his own did not receive him.' The rejection pattern established in Hosea reaches its culmination in the rejection of Christ. In both cases the rejected one is 'the good' that is thrown away.
4: Type — Kings installed without divine approval — John 19:15: 'We have no king but Caesar!' The moment Israel's leadership explicitly rejects YHWH's anointed King is the culmination of the pattern begun here: choosing rulers by human calculation rather than divine appointment. Jesus is the king installed by the Father's authority alone (Ps 2:6-7; Acts 2:36).
5: Theme — Calf-idol rejected — the fabricated image of the divine, fashioned by human hands and rejected by YHWH. Col 1:15: Christ is 'the image of the invisible God' — the true image, not fashioned by human hands but eternally and truly bearing the divine likeness. All human-made images of God fail; the incarnate Son does not.
6: Revelation of God — 'A craftsman made it; it is not God' — the idol's origin from human craft disqualifies it as God. The inverse is the incarnation: John 1:1-3 — the Word through whom all craftsmanship exists became flesh. The criterion 'a craftsman made it; it is not God' is precisely what the Son's eternal nature overcomes: he is not made; he is begotten.
7: Direct — 'They have sown the wind and shall reap the whirlwind' — Gal 6:7-8: 'Do not be deceived: God is not mocked. For whatever a man sows, that he will also reap. The one who sows to his own flesh will from the flesh reap corruption.' Paul's principle of harvest-judgment is drawn directly from this prophetic tradition.
8: Type — Israel swallowed up, worthless pottery among the nations — 1 Pet 2:7: Christ is the precious cornerstone — 'but for those who do not believe, the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone.' Against Israel-as-broken-pottery stands Christ-as-living-stone, and those united to him become living stones too (1 Pet 2:4-5).
9: Theme — Wild donkey going off alone, hiring lovers — the self-directed isolation of one who rejects YHWH's community and purchases foreign alliances. John 11:52: Christ dies 'to gather into one the children of God who are scattered.' The scattering impulse of Hosea's Israel is reversed by Christ who gathers the dispersed.
10: Direct — Gathering under burden of the king — Matt 11:28-30: 'Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest… my yoke is easy and my burden is light.' The contrast is deliberate: the gathering under foreign kings produces crushing burden; gathering to Christ produces rest. The same motif of gathering and burden-bearing, opposite outcomes.
11: Type — Multiplied altars that only produce more sin — Heb 10:11-12: 'Every priest stands daily ministering and offering the same sacrifices again and again, which can never take away sins. But this man… offered one sacrifice for sins for all time.' The multiplied altars of Hosea's Israel illustrate precisely the insufficiency of repeated sacrifice that the epistle to the Hebrews addresses.
12: Direct — 'Though I wrote for him the great things of my law, they were treated as something foreign' — Heb 8:10 (quoting Jeremiah 31:33): 'I will put my laws in their minds and write them on their hearts.' The new covenant promise directly responds to the failure here: since the external law was treated as foreign, Christ internalizes it through the Spirit.
13: Direct — 'The LORD takes no pleasure in them… their sacrifices' — Heb 10:6 (quoting Psalms 40:6): 'In burnt offerings and sin offerings you have taken no pleasure.' The pattern of rejected sacrifice in the prophets (Hosea, Amos, Isaiah) is theologically resolved by the Hebrews argument: the sacrifices fail not because of administration but because the wrong sacrifice — animals, not the obedient Son — was being offered.
14: Type — Israel forgot his Maker and built temples — John 2:19-21: Jesus declares his body to be the true Temple. Against the stone temples Israel built while forgetting the Maker stands the one in whom 'the fullness of God was pleased to dwell' (Col 1:19), the temple built by the Father, not by human hands.
